attributes.stem:
A 16-year-old boy is brought by his mother to the clinic with a 1-month history of bilateral ankle swelling. His mother also mentions that over the last year he often mishears what others say. There is no history of fever, rash, dizziness, palpitations, chest pain, shortness of breath, gastrointestinal disturbances nor recent travel. 

He has no other significant medical history. He does not take any regular medication, including illicit or recreational drug. Family history reveals that his grandfather has been on haemodialysis since the age of 45, and some of his uncles and aunts have been taking antihypertensives since they became young adults. 

On examination, his blood pressure is 155/90 mmHg, heart rate 80/min, respiratory rate 12/min and temperature 36.5oC. There is mild non-tender pitting oedema in both ankles up to the lower shins. The kidneys are non-ballotable. Lung fields are clear. Dual heart sounds with no murmur are heard. JVP is not elevated. Weber test does not lateralise to any side. Rinne test is positive for both ears. The results of urinalysis show haemoproteinuria.

His serum creatinine is 189 micromol/L (62-106 micromol/L), which correlates to an eGFR of 40 mL/min/1.73 m2. Audiometry shows high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in both ears. A renal biopsy is subsequently done to determine the cause of his condition.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following is the most likely histological/immunohistochemical finding in this patient's biopsy?
options: Mesangial proliferation with deposition of antigen-antibody complexes
Generalised thinning of the basement membrane
Diffuse podocyte effacement on electron microscope
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with kidney disease and sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL), with a family history of kidney disease. The clinical picture raises a strong suspicion of Alport syndrome (also known as hereditary nephritis). It is a genetic disease with a mutation in gene(s) encoding Alpha IV collagen, a major constituent of glomerular basement membrane. It could be X-linked (the majority), autosomal recessive or autosomal dominant. Patients with Alport syndrome at the early stage may present with recurrent or persistent microscopic haematuria, or more commonly, an episode of gross haematuria after an upper respiratory tract infection. As the disease progresses, proteinuria and hypertension emerge and end-stage renal disease would develop in the majority during their early adulthood. Alport syndrome is classically associated with high-frequency SNHL and anterior lenticonus. A strong family history would also be present. Histologically, the glomerular basement membrane is generally thinned and laminated. A basket weave pattern in the lamina densa of the basement membrane can be seen

Mesangial proliferation with deposition of antigen-antibody complexes is often found in mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis (GN), most commonly IgA nephropathy. IgA nephropathy is characterised by synpharyngitic macroscopic haematuria, but not hearing loss. In children (commonly aged 2-8) with Henoch-Scholein purpura, a condition associated with IgA nephropathy, arthritis and abdominal pain may also be present.

The lamina densa has thin and thick areas, but in Alport syndrome, the entire GBM is generally thin with an irregular multi-laminated appearance.

Crescentic GN is a result of rapidly progressing GN. The causes are usually ANCA vasculitis, Anti-GBM disease, post-streptococcal GN and Lupus nephritis. 

Diffuse podocyte effacement is seen in minimal change disease, a main cause of nephrotic syndrome in children. It is usually abrupt in onset and less associated with haematuria and SNHL.
learningPoints: Classic presentation of Alport syndrome (hereditary nephritis) includes early-onset kidney disease, SNHL and lenticonus.
Histologically, in Alport syndrome, the glomerular basement membrane is thinned and laminated.
references: Jais JP, Knebelmann B, Giatras I, De Marchi M, Rizzoni G, Renieri A, et al. X-linked Alport Syndrome: Natural History in 195 Families and Genotype- Phenotype Correlations in Males. Journal of the American Society of Nephrology. 2000;11(4):649-57. doi: 10.1681/ASN.V114649
Gubler MC. Inherited diseases of the glomerular basement membrane. Nature Clinical Practice Nephrology. 2008;4:24+. doi: 10.1038/ncpneph0671
